lesson: Distinguish hashing (one-way, for integrity/passwords), encryption (two-way, needs a key), and encoding like Base64 (no secrecy at all) before choosing a primitive.
Never store passwords with a fast general-purpose hash like SHA-256 or MD5 alone; use a deliberately slow KDF such as argon2id or bcrypt so brute-forcing costs attackers real time.
Generate a unique random salt per password via a CSPRNG and store it with the hash; a shared or missing salt lets attackers precompute one rainbow table for every user.
Compare secrets like password hashes, HMACs, or API tokens with a constant-time function (hmac.compare_digest, crypto.timingSafeEqual), never `==`, to close timing side-channels.
Never reuse a nonce or IV with the same key under AES-GCM or CTR mode; reuse lets an attacker XOR two ciphertexts to recover plaintext or forge valid auth tags.
Never invent a cipher, mode, or padding scheme; use audited libraries (libsodium, OpenSSL) and standard constructions like AES-GCM or ChaCha20-Poly1305 instead of rolling your own crypto.
Benchmark your KDF's cost parameter against current hardware to target roughly 250ms per hash, and periodically bump it as CPUs get faster, re-hashing on the user's next successful login.
Use authenticated encryption (AEAD) rather than encrypt-then-roll-your-own-MAC; plain CBC without an integrity check is vulnerable to padding-oracle attacks that recover plaintext byte by byte.
Encode the algorithm, version, and cost parameters inside the stored hash string itself (e.g. $argon2id$v=19$m=...) so verification is self-describing and old hashes upgrade without breaking.
